sentences.text: Well--
It was not mostly the Polish.
We-- I went close to the ghetto, people that lived close to the ghetto.
And they was only Ukrainian.
And they used to be our neighbors.
We used to-- I used to bring him some tablecloth sometimes, and in exchange for food for potatoes, a piece of bread, and things like that.
And then-- when I was caught, and I got a caught, they took everything away from me.
Yeah.
Only clothes, whatever-- it was-- clothes, most of it, nothing else.
We packed it up, anyway.
I don't know.
I had no money.
I don't remember.
The Ukrainian?
Before the war, or after the war?
When I was in the ghetto, we didn't see any Ukrainian people.
Oh, I hope that-- that-- that nobody saw me.
Oh no, they didn't make fun.
No.
The people were very nice.
Well, we knew-- I went to the people that we-- we knew.
We didn't-- I didn't go to any other.
Like for instance, my-- from my house, the-- the back of the house was already Ukrainian people that lived there.
But it didn't happen too often, you know, it was just once in a blue moon.
I have no idea.
I assume so.
But I cannot tell anything about other people.
Most likely I think so, yeah.
I was the-- the gutsy one, the risky one, [LAUGHS] too.
Maybe she did.
Yeah, she did.
She did I would say.
I came home with the potatoes or so.
If you're hungry, nothing stops-- stops anybody.
I mean, God forbid if you-- you should know from that.
If you're hungry, you do what you can to be-- to get something to eat.
I-- I-- I can only tell you, with-- I-- I was with my-- I saw some of my friends.
We were close.
And I didn't think there was no-- about what kind of behavior there was in other parts-- houses or so.
The house we-- we lived was fine.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We all had the same kind-- we all lived the same life.
As a matter of fact, one of-- one of my mother's relatives who lived in the house, she was-- she died in that house from starvation, or she was a little sick too.
But together, she died.
Yeah, big, all around, yeah.
Well, they were.
Yeah, there was-- you cannot-- when I went out, I crawled under.
There were certain little things that you could go under the fence on your stomach. you just crawled out.
You just-- I didn't go over the fence.
They was Jewish.
German was-- there were Germans, there was the Ukrainian police.
But the ghetto I think was all the Jewish police and Jewish-- this was like a little, you know, a little government, but by itself.
But-- under influence-- influence from the German.
Whatever they said that had to be done.
It wasn't just like, you know, a government.
Whatever was-- what they-- the German told, there was like a Gemeinde, you know, like what is an office.
And they-- they told the office what to do, come to send away today, who goes tomorrow, to these, to places to work, and things like that.
We had to go out to work.
That's right.
Yeah, the decisions-- the decisions, the Germans tell them what to do.
And they made the decisions, like, you know, the person and the job.
Even I went, as a little-- a young kid.
And even younger kids than I, when they were making a-- building a street from, you know, like cable-- cable-- cable stones.
Cobblestones you call it.
The people had to prepare, had to dig out it, you know, make-- prepare the-- the street to be able to put the cobblestones or so.
Also the bridge, or anything that had to be cleaned in this-- in town.
They took only the ghetto people.
I know very little of it.
I don't-- I really can't tell you much.
I was-- I wasn't interested in--
My mother?
I don't think she worked.
She-- maybe she did.
My brother wasn't in the-- with the men's side yet, because he was too young.
Anybody, anybody who could work, and two-- had two hands.
Babies didn't work.
But others, like four years old, five years old, they send them to work.
Same, whatever they told me to do.
We were groups.
They took us always in groups, not like just one person.
That was done outside the ghetto.
In the ghetto [LAUGHS] there was-- that was really outside the ghetto, when they took us to work.
No, it was-- the whole town is not-- wasn't big.
So streets, like a street away from the ghetto, two streets, some center of the-- of the-- of the city, or the town rather, that they needed it.
That was they send us.
I assume it was supposed-- it had to be done.
They wanted it.
Because we-- in a small town, we really had-- didn't have any-- we had streets, but they were not, not paved or anything.
So they wanted to improve.
They wanted-- they wanted-- you know, the Germans, they thought they're probably going to be there forever.
Of course, the Jewish people didn't-- were not in the-- in consideration.
Right, exactly.
Oh, yes.
I think it was like, 8 o'clock or so curfew.
We did.
I don't know, I really don't know.
Everybody was scared.
I assume everybody did what they-- what we was told to do.
No school.
This was no, no.
No school.
I was with my parents, with my mother and with the children outside in the ghetto.
And in the ghetto, we did-- what like what kids are doing, play, drink, walk around, talk.
Not that I know.
Not that I know.
I mean here was the problem.
We didn't go to-- to shul.
The shul was there, but nobody went.
It wasn't used as a shul.
I assume so.
I don't know.
I don't think we came to the holidays.
When they were-- we were-- we were in the ghetto for maybe nine months or so.
We went into the ghetto-- it was the beginning of the summer, I think.
And-- and-- and the ghetto finished was like in September.
We stayed a very-- a very short time in the ghetto.
Well, when-- it was cool already when-- as a matter of fact, when I took-- well, I will get to it, I guess at a--
No.
Say what you want to say.
Hmm?
I-- when I jump from the attic, I had my mother's winter jacket on.
I don't know how I put it on me.
Don't ask me how come I had it on me.
But I did.
And that's saved-- that's how I lived through the winter.
Oh, there was-- we slept all-- the whole family slept together.
Right.
Mother and the children, all together.
We were sleeping in the kitchen.
Whatever we had, the winter clothes.
We didn't have much clothes to begin with.
Whatever it was.
That's why my mother had the jacket.
It was-- her-- her coat.
Well, the-- the guard-- the main person their name was Wachs-- Wachs.
W-A-S-- Wachs, or a G?
Yeah, Wachs.
Oh, I think he was middle-- middle age, maybe [INAUDIBLE].
I suppose so.
But I-- to tell you the truth, I really don't know.
I think it's-- that's the way it was.
But if it's-- I really don't know.
I really don't know.
Only the time when they liquidated the ghetto, I think they were there.
But otherwise, I don't think so.
I don't know.
That was-- that was after the liberation.
Yeah.

Well, the-- the rumors were going around that they will-- that it-- what happened in other towns, cities that they take people and they-- they take them from the ghetto, and they bring-- take them to the-- to massive graves.
As a matter of fact, my-- my father, the day they took from our ghetto the men to Kostopol.
And--
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
And they-- they dig the graves for other-- for people from other areas.
Then when-- after a while, they sent the group, which my father was in, back to the ghetto.
And they took another group of men, and send to Kostopol.
Then the other group came back after a few weeks, and my father's group went back.
And that was the last time I saw my father.
And this went-- this is where when they dig the graves for us, for the Stepan ghetto, and all around, a little.
I never saw the grave, because I ran away.
So at that time, when-- when it started, when it came to our-- our-- to liquidate our ghetto, the night before I worked as a-- I was babysitting for a Ukrainian family at that time.
Outside of the ghetto?
Outside the ghetto, yeah.
This went through the-- legally-- I did not sneak out to do that.
And the-- they came that day in the afternoon.
They came to us to-- to ask for horse and buggy.
[INAUDIBLE] yeah.
And my-- the family that I babysit for, they had-- they were farmers.
And they worked on the farm, and not too far from the house.
So I said, no.
I don't know anything.
And that was Ukrainian people.
And I don't know anything, but the people on the farm.
So I guess they went there, and they told them for why they need it.
And then they came back, and they send me home.
And then-- and that was the end of it.
I never went back.
I mean that-- that was the night when they came, and they-- that-- that liquidating-- they liquidated our ghetto.

So again, we in the house, we-- me and my-- and my-- and my mother and my sisters and brothers, and other people, the people that lived in that house decided to go up in the attic, and take the-- we had like a-- to going up to the attic, we had a-- a--
A ladder.
And we took the ladder with us, so in case they're gonna-- they're gonna-- gonna come up, they'll see we don't-- that nobody is there.
But-- and also we knew from others in other towns that if-- let's say it happened one night, and whoever survived that night-- they-- they didn't bother anymore.
They lived another lot-- not-- a little bit longer.
And then they did it again.
So that's why we did this.
We went all-- all, the whole house, the people in the house, went up-- in the attic.
And we were laying all through the night, hoping that the morning will come, and they will-- they will stop.
They'll be finished, right.
They'll be finished.
Meanwhile, this really didn't happen in our town, in my town.
They-- they-- because of-- in the morning, we were-- we heard the gestapo speaking.
They spoke in-- in German.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
Jews, yeah.
Because if you're not going to go-- come out, we will-- we'll find you.
We'll shoot you on the spot.
And they were-- there was shooting going around all through there, crying, and-- and killing, and shooting.
And we was just stuck up there in the attic.
And finally, they came to our house.
They opened the door.
And they says, any Juden?
Any Juden?
Is there any Jewish people there?
And we didn't say nothing.
We said nothing.
Then they said, I know.
Where's-- where's the ladder?
Where's the ladder from the-- from to go up there?
I know you must be there.
So finally we look down, and that was in the barn, that was like a-- and we looked up-- down.
And there we saw the gestapo, two guys, I saw.
I remember the brown uniforms.
And maybe there was more.
And we started-- and I and my mother stood right there, looking down.
And we started-- they started to put-- to move to lower the-- the ladder down.
Who started to lower the ladder?
The people from up-- from the-- from us.
And for them to come up, or to us to come down rather.
They wanted us down.
They wanted to kill us.
We had to.
If not, they would come up themselves.
Yeah.
Yeah, they said they knew that the ladder is up.
The people must be there.
So they said, if you're not gonna do that, we're going to climb up.
We'll find you anyway.
So they started-- I don't know even remember who started to move the ladder down.
And I stood next to my mother, and in a spare moment, I-- something came into my mind.
And I left my mother, and I jumped to the other side of the house.
There was the [? sukkah. ?]
So I jumped, and after me jumped another-- the young girl, at that time a young-- year younger than I was, and my mother's cousin, a woman maybe in her early 30s.
Do you remember the names of the people that you were with?
Well, yeah.
The one is Perl, and the other one is Sassel.
But she never-- she didn't survive.
Only Perl and I--
Excuse me, no.
No.
And then we came out to the-- outside in the street.
And we saw nobody there.
And we decided to go to climb over the fence, and run-- and to run away.
And that's exactly what we did.
I did.
I started to go.
I was the first one.
We got out.
Then that was Ukraine-- the Ukrainian side.
Naturally the Ukrainian, there was children and adults watching what's going on in the ghetto.
They started to chase us, the-- the kids.
And they started to yell, there-- there comes the gestapo.
And make arrest, police, Jewish people, Jewish kids running!
And we kept running, and we kept on running.
And we decided we'll go to the cemetery.
So we went to the Jewish cemetery.
And when we got there, the same thing was there happening.
Everybody was-- there's crying and shooting was going on.
So we decided not to go into the cemetery, and go-- we were going into further into the fields, to the farm-- where the farmers lived.

And finally it started-- we find-- there at that time, they were cut-- they were-- that was the time when they were cutting the corn and the wheat, and they put such-- they made such a things heaped, you know, for this to dry.
And we went into this.
And each one was like separately.
And we-- and then we went into a barn.
And then some-- during the night, I hear people walking and yelling, Ukrainian, the police.
We police, if there's anybody there, out.
Otherwise, we'll kill you.
So we just-- we-- the three of us, we-- we were crawling on our stomach out from the barn.
And we went further into the-- again, into the fields.
And for some reason we were lucky, they didn't see us.
They didn't hear anything.
And then day came, daytime came.
And we got up, out of [INAUDIBLE], and we started to walk to look for a place where there's no Ukrainian people, where the Polish people live.
And this-- and then they met two more, two other kids, boys, who-- who-- and we teamed up.
And we all were walking together, just walking in the middle of the streets, didn't know who-- the people didn't know us.
The kids didn't know us.
They didn't say-- we just walked.
And then-- and then we-- until we reached the Polish, Wirke, that town.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, I believe, if not for-- if the Ukrainians wouldn't team up with the Germans, more people would survive.
That-- yeah, the Ukrainians.
Oh, yeah one is Mikhal, and he's in Israel, and has a beautiful family.
And the other one is Lazer.
Lazer-- that's-- or Lazer, whatever.
He's-- I never saw him.
But he was, I think, Lazer I believe is in Canada somewhere.
But that's what Mikhal told me.
But Perl is in-- is in Israel.
Perl, my friend-- the girl.
Perl, like Perl.
You know, we call Perl.
She lives-- she was-- she's married, has two children.
Her husband died by now.
And Mikhal is there.
Mikhal was my best friend, his family, you know.
At that time it was-- it was Pieczenik.
Pieczenik is like-- no.
I can write it down, if you want it from me.
We have everything in writing.
We didn't spell in Poland.
You want me to-- do you want see--
Pieczenik?
Oh, it doesn't matter.
And--
Now his name is, I think his name is altogether different.
It's not Pieczenik.
Yes, he picked his Israeli name.
I know.
I forgot at this minute.
I never saw him again.
From Stepan?
No, no.
See with Pieczenik, we knew better than the other family.
We all knew his parents too.
Everybody knew each other, you know.
Oh, yes.
Oh yeah, yeah.
That's for sure.
Then rumors-- what when we finally-- when we started to hide-- and the-- we were hiding in this forest.
And people-- people that lived close to the forest, they-- we used to go to ask for food.
And every day we would move to a different place.
And this lady happened to be-- that she couldn't be with us children.
Because she was--
The lady, my cousin's-- I said that my mother's cousin who jumped.
She went daylight, in daylight, to beg for food.
And they were-- and they corrupted her, and that killed her.
No, she was in her early 30s.
No idea.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, it was-- I don't know if that's about-- it was kilometers-- I don't know how.
But it was a little far.
Yeah.
We walked all day.
We met four kids in-- in Wirke.
We were hiding in the-- a part of in the forest that was--
Wirke, the town of the-- that was the town that we-- actually the forest that we--
Yeah.
So we met them, and they wanted to-- we and [INAUDIBLE], and we were all like teaming up together.
And then rumors were going around in town, in Wirke, that there are four Jewish kids hiding.
And they were after us.
But somehow, by moving from every day to different areas, they couldn't-- they didn't caught up with us.
But that day, when we met that-- the four other boys from a difference town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were sitting together.
And I said to them, you know what?
That's not a good idea to sit together.
Because I know they know there-- there's four kids.
Where were you sitting?
Where was this?
Where were you located?
In the forest.
Yeah.
And so they separated, and we all-- like not too far.
But they caught them.
All of a sudden--
Yeah.
All of a sudden we hear walking steps, you know.
And it was-- you-- in the forest you hear, if somebody walks, you hear walking.
And we took a peek, like behind the-- in between the-- the bushes.
And I saw-- I saw the four boys and the policeman walking with them, after them with a gun, with a-- with a--
You know, what is a big-- the rifle, the rifle.
And they killed them.
They were gone.
No, no.
And then we--
No, no.
No.
It was like September time.
To me, it's-- I think it was September, maybe it's beginning of October.
But it's something like this, you know.
Because right after, winter came.
And we had-- we had-- we couldn't sleep in-- in the forest.
So this Polish lady gave us-- let us into the house once in a while.
Not all the time, but--
Yeah.
And some others did too.
Some others did too, let us in, or they lets us sleep in the barn or so.
Because it was very dangerous for them to let us in.
Because they knew if they-- if anybody was hiding Jewish people, they'll be-- they'll kill them, just the same as the Jewish.
So they were really--
Strangers, completely.
Well my Polish family, who survived it, is-- is-- was Janicki.
OK.
I have the--
OK.
